fact: The Byford Dolphin diving bell accident occurred in 1983 on the semi-submersible drilling rig Byford Dolphin in the North Sea
The divers were pressurized to nine atmospheres, equivalent to approximately 300 metres of seawater depth
source: Report on the Byford Dolphin accident, Norwegian Petroleum Directorate (1983); also summarized in Wikipedia — Byford Dolphin

fact: The accident was caused by a diving tender opening the clamp connecting the diving bell to the transfer chamber before the bell was sealed, causing explosive decompression from 9 atm to 1 atm
source: NOU 1983:53 — Report of the Investigation Commission for the Diving Accident on Byford Dolphin on 5 November 1983, Norwegian Government

fact: The explosive decompression forced one diver's body through a narrowly opened trunk door, causing massive physical trauma
source: Forensic autopsy report by Dr. Øystein Eidsvik and colleagues, published as part of the Norwegian investigation; referenced in Wikipedia — Byford Dolphin

fact: The investigation found no mechanical malfunction — the accident resulted from human error in prematurely opening the clamp
source: NOU 1983:53 — Norwegian Government Investigation Commission report on the Byford Dolphin diving accident
